aliases: Charcoal Grilled Lamb Chops Indian Style

english: Charcoal Grilled Lamb Chops Indian Style

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lamb chop entry; no specific tandoori-marinated variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific tandoori lamb chop code. A00GJ (lamb meat, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for tandoori lamb chops; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Tandoori Lamb Chops is approximately 200 g, typically comprising two bone-in rib chops as served in North Indian restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Lamb, domestic, rib, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8" fat, cooked, roasted (NDB 17082) and ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / ICMR-National Institute of Nutrition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (protein, fat, carbohydrate, energy) anchored to USDA FoodData Central lamb rib cooked values (NDB 17082). Micronutrients cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2024 edition). Marinade contribution (yogurt, spices) adds minor carbohydrate and sodium; values adjusted accordingly. Saturated fat estimated from USDA lamb rib fatty acid profile. Input AI-generated values reviewed against USDA and ICMR-NIN; values are consistent and retained with minor rounding.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for high-protein, low-carbohydrate grilled meat
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tandoori lamb chops. GI estimated at 15 based on analogous high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate grilled meat entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care). Carbohydrate content is 1.5 g per 100 g; glycemic load per 200 g serving = (15 × 3.0) / 100 = 0.45, rounded to 0.5. GI is effectively not applicable as a dietary concern given negligible carbohydrate load.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (350 mg per 100 g; 700 mg per standard 200 g serving) from marinade salt and spice rubs
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or fewer, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium accompaniments such as chutneys or pickles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (1.5 g per 100 g) and high protein (18.5 g per 100 g) support glycemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tandoori lamb chops are suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions (100–150 g) as part of a balanced meal; the negligible carbohydrate load produces minimal postprandial glucose excursion, though total caloric density should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (approximately 6.2 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (~72 mg per 100 g) from lamb rib, combined with elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional servings of 100 g, prefer leaner cuts (leg or loin) over rib chops, and ensure the overall dietary pattern remains low in saturated fat and sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat and high-heat charcoal grilling may promote pro-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consume tandoori lamb chops only occasionally and consider substituting with chicken or fish tandoori preparations, which carry a lower pro-inflammatory burden.

gout.key_factor: High purine content in red meat elevates serum uric acid
gout.guidance: Individuals with gout or hyperuricemia should avoid or strictly limit tandoori lamb chops and consult a dietitian regarding safe red-meat intake thresholds.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Lamb Chops are a premium North Indian and Mughlai dish in which bone-in lamb rib chops undergo a two-stage marinade process: an initial tenderising rub of ginger-garlic paste and lemon juice, followed by a rich coating of thick yogurt, kashmiri chili powder, garam masala, and crushed dried fenugreek leaves (kasuri methi). The marinated chops are skewered and cooked at extremely high temperatures in a clay tandoor oven over charcoal, producing a characteristic exterior char, deep smoky aroma, and a juicy, tender interior. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 220 kcal, 18.5 g protein, 15 g fat (of which ~6.2 g is saturated), and only 1.5 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate option. Sodium is elevated at 350 mg per 100 g due to the spiced marinade. The dish is rich in zinc (4.5 mg), vitamin B12 (2.5 mcg), niacin (6.5 mg), and selenium (22 mcg), reflecting the nutritional profile of cooked lamb. Glycemic index is estimated at 15 (low), with a negligible glycemic load per serving. Clinically, the dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management in moderate portions but warrants caution for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and saturated fat content. It is a centrepiece dish for festive meals, restaurant dining, and special occasions across North India, Pakistan, and the broader South Asian diaspora.

confidence_reason: Core macros are consistent with USDA FoodData Central lamb rib cooked values and ICMR-NIN reference data. Saturated fat, cholesterol, and fatty acid breakdown are derived from USDA lamb rib fatty acid profile. Micronutrients from input are plausible and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for tandoori lamb chops exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Water content is not available from the consulted sources for this specific preparation.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (6.2 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (6.1 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.1 g), trans_fat_g (0.3 g), and cholesterol_mg (72 mg) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb rib cooked fatty acid profile (NDB 17082) and are not directly reported in the input; these values are plausible for bone-in rib chops with some fat retained. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for tandoori-grilled lamb chops is not separately reported in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN for this specific preparation as of 2026. 3. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate grilled meat entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for tandoori lamb chops is published. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = 0.5 (negligible). 4. The input contained three duplicate portion entries (all '1 serving 200g, is_default true'); deduplicated to a single 200 g default serving. 5. Halal flag set to true as lamb is permissible under Islamic dietary law when slaughtered according to halal practice; consumers should verify halal certification at point of purchase. 6. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for tandoori-marinated lamb chops specifically.
